title: Gift Tax Fundamentals
description: Understand the gift tax annual exclusion, calculate taxable gifts, apply gift-splitting rules, and identify gift tax exclusions.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: The gift tax exists to prevent avoidance of estate tax through lifetime transfers. A gift is a transfer of property where full consideration is not received in return. Understanding gift tax rules is essential for effective wealth transfer planning.

sections.title: Annual Exclusion (2026)
sections.content: $19,000 per donee per year. Key features: Unlimited number of donees; must be "present interest"; per-donee, not aggregate limit.

sections.title: Gift Splitting Example
sections.content:
Married couple with 3 children:
2 donors × 3 donees × $19,000 = $114,000/year can be transferred gift-tax-free.

Gift splitting allows married couples to treat each gift as made half by each spouse. Requirements: Both spouses must consent; gift-splitting election on Form 709; applies to all gifts made that year.

Example: Wife gives $38,000 to son. With gift-splitting:
• Wife: $19,000 (her half) ✓ Covered by exclusion
• Husband: $19,000 (his half) ✓ Covered by exclusion
• No taxable gift!

sections.title: Present vs. Future Interest
sections.content:
Present Interest (Qualifies for Annual Exclusion): Immediate right to use, possess, or enjoy; unrestricted access to gift.

Future Interest (No Annual Exclusion): Right to use property comes later; most trust gifts are future interests.

sections.title: Crummey Powers
sections.content:
Crummey powers convert future interest to present interest for annual exclusion. How it works:
1. Gift made to trust
2. Beneficiary notified of withdrawal right (typically 30-60 days)
3. Beneficiary allows right to lapse
4. Gift qualifies for annual exclusion

sections.title: Unlimited Exclusions (Not Subject to Gift Tax)
Exam Warning
sections.content: Direct Payment Requirement: Tuition and medical payments must be made directly to the provider—not to the student/patient. Paying $50,000 tuition directly to Harvard: No gift tax ✓. Giving grandson $50,000 to pay his tuition: Taxable gift!

sections.title: Calculating Taxable Gifts Formula
sections.content: Taxable Gifts = Total Gifts - Annual Exclusions - Marital Deduction - Charitable Deduction

sections.title: Lifetime Gift Tax Exemption
sections.content: Unified Credit (2026): $7.0 Million (TCJA sunset reduced from ~$13.6M). Same exemption for gifts and estate; lifetime gifts reduce estate exemption; gift tax rate: 40%.

sections.title: Why Give During Life?
items: Remove future appreciation from estate
Use annual exclusions ($19,000/year/person)
Leverage valuation discounts
Pay tax on tax-exclusive basis

sections.title: Tax-Exclusive vs. Tax-Inclusive
sections.content:
Gift Tax (Tax-Exclusive): Tax paid on amount transferred only; gift tax paid by donor is removed from estate.

Estate Tax (Tax-Inclusive): Tax calculated on assets including tax payment; more expensive than gift tax.

Example: Transfer $1M at 40% rate:
• Gift: Pay $400,000 tax, $1M transfers (donor pays tax from other assets)
• Estate: $1.4M needed to transfer $1M after 40% tax

sections.title: Mnemonic: TEMP
sections.content: Unlimited Gift Exclusions: Tuition (direct), Education medical (direct), Marital, Political/charities

sections.title: Summary
items: Annual exclusion is $19,000/donee (2026) for present interest gifts
Gift splitting doubles the annual exclusion for married couples
Tuition and medical paid directly to providers are unlimited exclusions
Crummey powers convert trust gifts to present interests
Gift tax is "tax-exclusive" making it more efficient than estate tax

knowledgeCheck.question: John and Mary are married and decide to utilize gift splitting. They have two children, Sarah and Tom. They also wish to make a gift to a trust for the benefit of their grandchild, Emily. What is the maximum amount John and Mary can gift tax-free in one year utilizing gift splitting and the annual exclusion?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: With gift splitting, the annual exclusion is doubled. They can gift $38,000 to Sarah and Tom ($19,000 x 2 donors x 2 donees = $76,000). The gift to the trust for Emily is considered a future interest and does not qualify for the annual exclusion. Therefore, the total is $76,000 + $0 = $76,000. The question asks for the *maximum* amount, so the correct answer is $114,000. ($19,000 * 2 donors * 3 donees). Note the trust gift does NOT qualify for the exclusion.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to consider gift splitting and how many donees qualify for the annual exclusion. Also, trusts are often future interests.

reveal.question: List the requirements for utilizing gift splitting.
reveal.answer: 1. Both spouses must consent to split gifts. 2. The spouses must be married at the time of the gift. 3. Both spouses must be U.S. citizens or resident aliens. 4. The spouse who is not the actual donor cannot make any gifts to the recipient of the split gift. 5. The election to gift split must be made on Form 709 (United States Gift (and Generation-Skipping Transfer) Tax Return). 6. Gift splitting applies to all gifts made by either spouse during the calendar year.

rows.label: Definition
values: Immediate right to use, possess, or enjoy property.
Right to use, possess, or enjoy property at some point in the future.

rows.label: Annual Exclusion
values: Qualifies for the annual gift tax exclusion.
Does not qualify for the annual gift tax exclusion.

rows.label: Example
values: Unrestricted gift of cash or securities directly to an individual.
Gift to a trust where the beneficiary cannot access the funds until a future date.

title: Estate Tax Calculation
description: Calculate the gross estate, identify available deductions, apply the unified credit, and understand portability.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Estate tax applies to the value of a decedent's taxable estate above the exemption amount. Understanding what's included in the gross estate, available deductions, and the unified credit system is essential for CFP professionals.

sections.title: Gross Estate Rule
sections.content: General Rule (IRC §2033-2044): Include all property in which decedent had an interest at death.

sections.title: Gross Estate Components
rows: Incidents of ownership or payable to estate

sections.title: Life Insurance Rules (§2042)
sections.content:
Life insurance is included if decedent:
• Had "incidents of ownership" (ability to change beneficiary, borrow, etc.)
• Policy payable to estate
• Transferred policy within 3 years of death

sections.title: Joint Tenancy (§2040)
sections.content:
Between spouses: 50% included regardless of contribution.
Between non-spouses: Proportionate to contribution (burden on estate to prove).

sections.title: Estate Tax Deductions
sections.content:
Marital Deduction (Unlimited): Property passing to surviving US citizen spouse; must be included in gross estate; must pass to spouse (outright or qualifying trust).

Qualifying Trusts: QTIP, GPOA trust, Estate trust.

Charitable Deduction (Unlimited): Property passing to qualified charities.

Debts, Expenses, Losses: Mortgages and debts; funeral expenses; administrative costs; casualty/theft losses.

State Death Taxes: Deduction for state estate or inheritance taxes paid.

sections.title: Estate Tax Calculation Formula
sections.content:
Tentative Tax Base = Gross Estate - Deductions + Adjusted Taxable Gifts

Estate Tax = Tentative Tax - Credits

sections.title: Unified Credit (2026)
sections.content: Exempts $7.0 million from estate/gift tax (TCJA sunset). Credit amount: ~$2,725,800 (40% of $7.0M). Reduced by gift tax credits used during life.

sections.title: Portability: Deceased Spousal Unused Exclusion (DSUE)
sections.content:
Concept: Surviving spouse can use deceased spouse's unused exemption.

Requirements:
• Must file estate tax return (Form 706)
• Election must be timely made
• Surviving spouse must be US citizen

Example:
• Husband dies with $5M estate, $7.0M exemption
• Unused: $7.0M - $5M = $2.0M
• Wife's new exemption: $7.0M + $2.0M = $9.0M

sections.title: Exam Warning
sections.content: Portability Limitations: Only available from last deceased spouse; does NOT apply to GST exemption; must file Form 706 return even if no tax due to preserve portability.

sections.title: State Estate/Inheritance Taxes
sections.content:
Estate Tax: Tax on the estate (transferor pays).
Inheritance Tax: Tax on beneficiary (recipient pays).

Many states have lower exemptions than federal: Massachusetts ($2M), Oregon ($1M), New York ($6.94M with cliff).

Planning Consideration: Federal exemption may shelter estate, but state tax can still apply.

sections.title: Mnemonic: MCE
sections.content: Estate Deductions: Marital, Charitable, Expenses/debts

sections.title: Summary
items: Gross estate includes all property with incidents of ownership
Marital and charitable deductions are unlimited
Unified credit exempts $7.0M (2026 - TCJA sunset) from estate tax
Portability allows surviving spouse to use deceased spouse's unused exemption
State estate taxes may apply even when no federal tax is due

sections.title: Life Insurance and the Gross Estate

knowledgeCheck.question: Harold transferred a life insurance policy on his life to an Irrevocable Life Insurance Trust (ILIT) five years ago, naming his children as beneficiaries. Harold retained no rights to the policy. However, two years ago, Harold's business partner, Fred, transferred a life insurance policy on Harold's life to Harold, and Harold named his estate as the beneficiary. Harold died this year. Which life insurance policy, if any, is included in Harold's gross estate?
options: Only the policy owned by the ILIT.
Only the policy naming Harold's estate as beneficiary.
Both policies are included in Harold's gross estate.
Neither policy is included in Harold's gross estate.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The policy owned by the ILIT is not included because Harold transferred it more than three years before his death and retained no incidents of ownership. The policy naming Harold's estate as beneficiary IS included because it is payable to the estate.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the 3-year rule and incidents of ownership.

reveal.question: How is property held in joint tenancy with a non-spouse treated for estate tax purposes?
reveal.answer: The portion of the property attributable to the decedent's contribution is included in their gross estate. The burden of proof is on the estate to demonstrate the surviving tenant's contribution.

sections.title: Joint Tenancy Inclusion: Spouse vs. Non-Spouse

rows.label: Inclusion in Gross Estate
values: 50% of the property's value
Proportionate to the decedent's contribution (burden of proof on the estate)

rows.label: Contribution Consideration
values: Irrelevant; 50% inclusion regardless of contribution
Relevant; inclusion is based on the decedent's contribution

title: Generation-Skipping Transfer Tax
description: Understand the purpose of GSTT, identify skip persons and taxable transfers, and apply the GST exemption.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: The Generation-Skipping Transfer Tax (GSTT) prevents wealth from skipping generations and avoiding estate tax at each level. Without GSTT, a grandparent could transfer to grandchildren and bypass estate tax at the parent's generation.

sections.title: GSTT Rate
sections.content: 40% flat rate—applied IN ADDITION to gift or estate tax. This makes GSTT one of the most punitive taxes in the code.

sections.title: Skip Person Definition
sections.content:
A skip person is:
1. Two or more generations below transferor, OR
2. A trust with only skip person beneficiaries

Skip Persons include: Grandchildren (if parent alive or deceased); great-grandchildren; unrelated persons 37.5+ years younger.

Non-Skip Persons: Children; spouse; persons within one generation.

sections.title: Deceased Parent Exception
sections.content: If a grandchild's parent (transferor's child) is deceased, the grandchild "moves up" and is NOT a skip person. This is a key exam concept.

sections.title: Types of GST Transfers
sections.content:
Direct Skip: Transfer directly to skip person (gift to grandchild, bequest to grandchild). Tax paid by: Transferor (or estate).

Taxable Distribution: Distribution from trust to skip person (not direct skip). Trust pays income to grandchild. Tax paid by: Distributee.

Taxable Termination: Trust terminates, assets pass to skip persons. Life estate to child ends; remainder to grandchildren. Tax paid by: Trustee from trust assets.

sections.title: GST Exemption (2026)
sections.content:
$7.0 Million—same amount as estate/gift exemption (TCJA sunset). Key features:
• Separate allocation from estate exemption
• NOT portable (unlike estate exemption)
• Can be allocated to direct skips or trusts benefiting skip persons

sections.title: Inclusion Ratio Calculation
sections.content:
Inclusion Ratio = 1 - (GST Exemption Allocated ÷ Value of Transfer)

Applicable Rate = Maximum Rate × Inclusion Ratio

Example:
• Transfer $5M to dynasty trust
• Allocate $5M GST exemption
• Inclusion ratio: 1 - ($5M/$5M) = 0
• Applicable rate: 40% × 0 = 0%
• Result: Trust is "GST-exempt"

sections.title: GSTT Planning Strategies
items: Dynasty Trust: Allocate full GST exemption; trust lasts for generations; no estate tax or GSTT at each generation
Annual Exclusion: Direct skip gifts qualifying for annual exclusion are also exempt from GSTT
Reverse QTIP Election: Allows deceased spouse's executor to preserve deceased spouse's GST exemption

sections.title: Exam Warning
sections.content:
GSTT Traps:
• Inadequate exemption allocation = inclusion ratio of 1 = full 40% tax
• Non-portable exemption: GST exemption CANNOT be ported to surviving spouse
• Trust modifications may trigger GSTT if beneficiaries change

sections.title: Mnemonic: DDD
sections.content: GST Transfer Types: Direct skip, Distribution (taxable), Termination (taxable)

sections.title: Summary
items: GSTT is 40% flat tax on transfers to persons 2+ generations below
Skip persons include grandchildren and more remote descendants
GST exemption ($7.0M) is NOT portable between spouses
Deceased parent exception moves grandchild up a generation
Dynasty trusts use GST exemption to avoid transfer taxes for generations

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following transfers would be subject to the Generation-Skipping Transfer Tax (GSTT)?
options: A gift of $10,000 to a child.
A trust distribution to a spouse.
A direct gift of $20,000 to a grandchild whose parent is still living.
A bequest of $50,000 to a sibling.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A direct gift to a grandchild whose parent is still living is a direct skip and is subject to GSTT. The other options involve transfers to non-skip persons.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the definition of a skip person and whether the transfer is direct or indirect.

reveal.question: Name the three types of GST transfers.
reveal.answer: The three types of GST transfers are: 1) Direct Skip, 2) Taxable Distribution, and 3) Taxable Termination.

rows.label: Definition
values: A transfer directly to a skip person.
A distribution from a trust to a skip person.
The termination of a trust interest where property passes to a skip person.

rows.label: Example
values: Gift or bequest to a grandchild.
Trust income paid to a grandchild.
Trust assets distributed to grandchildren upon the death of the child-beneficiary.

rows.label: Tax Paid By
values: Transferor (or estate)
Distributee
Trust

title: Valuation and Special Techniques
description: Understand fair market value determination, apply valuation discounts, and explain special use valuation and alternate valuation.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Valuation is critical for gift and estate tax purposes. Understanding fair market value, available discounts, and special valuation elections can significantly reduce transfer tax liability.

sections.title: Fair Market Value Definition
sections.content: "The price at which property would change hands between a willing buyer and a willing seller, neither being under any compulsion to buy or sell and both having reasonable knowledge of relevant facts."

sections.title: Valuation Dates
sections.content:
Estate tax: Date of death (or alternate valuation date)
Gift tax: Date of gift

sections.title: Lack of Marketability Discount
sections.content:
Reflects inability to easily sell interest in secondary market.

Applies to: Closely-held business interests; private company stock; limited partnership interests.

Typical Range: 15-35%

sections.title: Lack of Control (Minority) Discount
sections.content:
Reflects inability to control business decisions.

Minority interests cannot: Elect board members; declare dividends; sell company assets; hire/fire management.

Typical Range: 15-40%

sections.title: Tax Savings from Discounts
sections.content: Tax Savings at 40%: ($1M - $600K) × 40% = $160,000. Discounts must be supported by qualified appraisal meeting USPAP standards.

sections.title: Special Use Valuation (IRC §2032A)
sections.content:
Purpose: Allow family farms and businesses to be valued at current use rather than highest/best use.

Requirements:
1. Property must be real property
2. Used for farming or closely-held business
3. Must meet percentage tests: 50% of adjusted gross estate is farm/business property; 25% is qualifying real property
4. Decedent or family must have materially participated
5. Property passes to qualified heirs
6. Qualified heirs must continue qualified use for 10 years

Maximum Reduction (2026): $1,440,000

Recapture: If property is sold or use changes within 10 years, special use valuation recaptured with interest.

sections.title: Alternate Valuation Date (IRC §2032)
sections.content:
Option: Value estate assets 6 months after death instead of date of death.

Requirements:
• Must reduce gross estate value
• Must reduce estate tax liability
• Election is irrevocable

When to Use: When assets have declined in value since death.

Example:
• Date of death value: $15,000,000
• 6-month value: $13,000,000
• Election saves: ($15M - $13M) × 40% = $800,000

Note: If property sold/distributed before 6 months, use date of disposition value.

sections.title: Other Valuation Discounts
sections.content:
Blockage Discount: Large blocks of stock may depress price if sold at once. Example: 500,000 shares at $50 = $25M market value, but blockage discount of 10% = $22.5M.

Key Person Discount: Value reduction when business depends heavily on a key person who died.

sections.title: Valuation Planning Techniques
items: Freeze Techniques: Lock in current value, shift future appreciation (GRATs, IDGT sales, preferred partnership interests)

Family Limited Partnerships: Transfer limited partnership interests; apply minority and marketability discounts; maintain control through general partner interest; shift appreciation to younger generations

sections.title: Mnemonic: MMM
sections.content: Valuation Discounts: Minority, Marketability, Must be supported by appraisal

sections.title: Summary
items: Fair market value is the standard for gift and estate tax
Minority and marketability discounts can significantly reduce taxable value
Special use valuation can save up to $1.44M for farms/businesses
Alternate valuation date requires both reduced value AND reduced tax
Proper appraisals are essential to support claimed discounts

knowledgeCheck.question: Mrs. Gable passed away on March 15, 2024. Her executor is considering using the alternate valuation date. What is the latest date the alternate valuation can be?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The alternate valuation date is six months after the date of death. Therefore, the latest date the alternate valuation can be is September 15, 2024.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the timeframe for the alternate valuation date.

sections.title: Test Yourself: Lack of Marketability Discount

reveal.question: Define the Lack of Marketability Discount and provide examples of the types of assets to which it commonly applies.
reveal.answer: The Lack of Marketability Discount reflects the reduced value of an asset due to its inability to be easily sold in a public market. It commonly applies to closely-held business interests, private company stock, and limited partnership interests.

sections.title: Comparison: Date of Death vs. Alternate Valuation Date

rows.label: General Rule
values: Asset valued as of the date of the decedent's death.
Asset valued six months after the date of death, or the date of disposition if earlier.

rows.label: Requirements for Use
values: Always available, unless alternate valuation is elected.
Can only be elected if it decreases the gross estate AND the estate tax liability.

rows.label: Impact on Asset Value
values: Reflects value at the time of death.
Reflects value six months later, potentially capturing market fluctuations.
